title: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(2) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.28' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, index=proxy, gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(4) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(4) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.28' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, index=proxy, gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as src_count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where earliest=-7d All_Traffic.action="allowed"
  by All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| eval dest_domain=replace(dest,"^\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$","")
| lookup gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv dest_domain OUTPUT primary_processor, approval_status
| where isnotnull(primary_processor) AND (approval_status!="approved" OR isnull(approval_status))
| sort - bytes_out
| table dest, dest_domain, primary_processor, approval_status, bytes_out, src_count
description: Processors must not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. This use case detects new destination domains, SaaS tenants, or infrastructure regions receiving personal data from approved processors — flagging unapproved sub-processing paths inferred from network and SaaS audit telemetry.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes proxy. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build allowlist of approved processor and sub-processor domains from DPAs; (2) classify egress traffic by processor parent using DNS and TLS SNI metadata; (3) alert on high-volume flows to unlisted destinations; (4) require procurement approval workflow to update allowlist; (5) document investigations for Art. 28 accountability. Indexes required: `index=proxy`; Lookups: `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist`, `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv`.
visualization: Table (unapproved destinations), Sankey (flow from segment to destination), Single value (new destinations this week), Bar chart (bytes by processor).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Standard sub-processor additions** — Changes to commonly-used sub-processors (e.g., CDN providers, email delivery services) listed in the processor's standard sub-processor register that fall within the pre-approved categories.

2. **Internal reorganisation renames** — Sub-processors that change legal entity names due to corporate restructuring without changing the actual processing may appear as new sub-processors.

3. **Notification latency** — Processors who notify sub-processor changes within the contractual notification period (typically 30 days) may show as changed before the controller's tracking system is updated.

4. **General authorisation model** — Under Art.28(2) general written authorisation, the processor may add sub-processors unless the controller objects within the notice period; alerts during the objection window are informational, not violations.

5. **Identical processing with entity change** — Acquisitions of sub-processors by other companies that continue identical processing may trigger change alerts without actual processing differences.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv')`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.28(2)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.28(2) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Build allowlist of approved processor and sub-processor domains from DPAs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** classify egress traffic by processor parent using DNS and TLS SNI metadata. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert on high-volume flows to unlisted destinations. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** require procurement approval workflow to update allowlist. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** document investigations for Art. 28 accountability. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events.

This search uses `| tstats` against a CIM-accelerated data model rather than reading raw events. The performance delta is typically 10-50x over raw search, and for large indexes can be >100x. The acceleration summaries are maintained on a rolling window (default: 1 year, configurable in the data model editor) and are queried from indexer-local summary files rather than re-scanning raw events.

CIM acceleration dependency: this search FAILS SILENTLY if the underlying data model is not accelerated. It will return zero rows instead of raising an error, which is exactly the wrong failure mode for a compliance detection. Verify acceleration status before scheduling:

```spl
| rest /services/admin/summarization
| search title="<datamodel_name>"
| table title access_count summary.complete summary.earliest_time summary.latest_time
```

`summary.complete` should show 100 (or very close). If it's below 95, the acceleration is still catching up or has stalled — check the DMA audit log (`index=_audit action=dma_run`) for errors.

Aggregation choice: `sum()` is grouped by `All_Traffic.dest`. This produces a summary row per distinct combination, which is typically what compliance reporting requires (one row per asset, one row per user, one row per rule). If downstream processing expects a flat event stream, avoid `tstats` — summary rows are not raw events and cannot be drilled into without re-running against the raw index.

When NOT to use tstats: if the field being filtered was added after the acceleration started, or is search-time extracted by an app the acceleration isn't aware of, the field will not exist in the summaries and the `where` clause will drop every row. Rebuild the acceleration after any field-extraction changes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Traffic` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Traffic` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.28: GDPR Sub-Processor Change Monitoring]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.28,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `approval_status`, `bytes_out`, `dest`, `dest_domain`, `primary_processor`, `src_count`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table approval_status, bytes_out, dest, dest_domain, primary_processor, src_count
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_processor_subprocessor_allowlist.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.28: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`unapproved destinations`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Sankey (`flow from segment to destination`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`new destinations this week`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 4 — Distribution (`bytes by processor`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Zero results when acceleration is fresh:** The field you're filtering on was likely added after the acceleration started — rebuild the acceleration.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We processors must not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Add a new sub-processor to a test processor's register without a corresponding notification event to the controller within the contractual notice period; verify the change monitoring detects the undisclosed change.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Add a new sub-processor with a notification event logged within 15 days (within the 30-day contractual notice period); verify no undisclosed change alert fires.

evidence: Sub-processor change log showing notification dates, controller acknowledgement timestamps, objection window status, and new sub-processor due diligence completion.
exclusions: Sub-processor changes within the Art.28(2) general authorisation model during the active objection window; entity name changes due to corporate restructuring without processing changes.
